ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for stir-fried lamb with scallions; closest_parent 'lamb dish' (FOODON_03302051) used as the nearest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Lamb and mutton meat dishes) is the closest parent category; no exact code exists for cong bao yang rou.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for this specific stir-fried lamb preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of stir-fried lamb with scallions is approximately 250 g, comprising thinly sliced lamb pieces and scallions served as a main dish, often accompanied by steamed rice.

nutrient_source.title: Lamb, composite of retail cuts, cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; supplemented with stir-fried lamb dish composition from USDA Foundation Foods and FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for Chinese stir-fried meat dishes
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for cong bao yang rou exists in any approved primary source. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked lamb (composite retail cuts, ~175 kcal/100g cooked) adjusted for stir-fry preparation with oil (~15 g added fat per 100g dish), soy sauce (sodium contribution ~400 mg/100g dish), and scallion dilution effect. The dish is approximately 70% lamb by weight after cooking. Energy, protein, fat, and carbohydrate values are estimated from ingredient composition. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; analogous estimate based on cooked lamb and non-starchy vegetable stir-fry category
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for cong bao yang rou. GI estimated at 15 based on the analogous category of cooked lamb (GI ~0 for pure protein-fat food) combined with scallions and minimal soy sauce carbohydrate contribution. The University of Sydney GI Database lists meat-based stir-fry dishes with non-starchy vegetables in the GI 10–20 range. GL per 250g serving = (15 × 3.2 × 2.5) / 100 = 1.2. Classified as low GI.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce (~520 mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-soy-sauce preparation, as a 250g serving delivers approximately 1300 mg sodium, representing over 50% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index (~15)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Stir-fried lamb with scallions has a low glycemic index of approximately 15 and a glycemic load of 1.2 per serving, making it a suitable protein-rich main dish for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, provided it is consumed without high-carbohydrate accompaniments such as white rice in large quantities.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from lamb and sodium from soy sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish contains approximately 4.2 g saturated fat per 100g and high sodium (~520 mg/100g); individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion size, choose leaner lamb cuts, and request low-sodium soy sauce to reduce cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-fried Lamb with Scallions (葱爆羊肉, cōng bào yángròu) is a classic Northern Chinese dish, particularly associated with Beijing and Inner Mongolian culinary traditions. The dish is prepared by flash-frying thinly sliced lamb at very high heat in a wok with generous quantities of scallions (spring onions), soy sauce, Shaoxing rice wine, sesame oil, ginger, and garlic. The cooking technique — known as 'bào' (爆), meaning explosive high-heat stir-frying — produces tender, slightly charred lamb with a savory, umami-rich flavour profile and a characteristic aroma from the caramelised scallions and sesame oil. The texture is tender and juicy with a slight chew from the lamb, contrasted by the soft, fragrant scallions. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (~17.5 g/100g), moderate in fat (~12.8 g/100g), and very low in carbohydrates (~3.2 g/100g), giving it a low glycemic index of approximately 15. It is a good source of vitamin B12, zinc, iron, and niacin from the lamb. The sodium content is elevated due to soy sauce (~520 mg/100g), making it a concern for individuals with hypertension. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, the dish is well-suited as a low-GI, high-protein main course. In Singapore, this dish is served in Northern Chinese and Beijing-style restaurants and is priced at the premium end of the Chinese restaurant market.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for cong bao yang rou (stir-fried lamb with scallions) was found in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA FoodData Central as a prepared dish). Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked lamb data combined with stir-fry preparation adjustments. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database category for meat-based stir-fries with non-starchy vegetables. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy cooked lamb (composite retail cuts) adjusted for stir-fry oil addition and soy sauce sodium contribution; no exact approved-source entry exists for this dish. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy lamb composite entry and is not expected to be significant in this preparation. 3. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for cong bao yang rou was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. 4. Halal status is marked false because lamb sourcing and preparation halal certification cannot be confirmed for the specific restaurant (See Ya Tonight, Singapore); consumers requiring halal-certified food should verify with the restaurant directly. 5. The price of SGD 36.40 from field survey data (See Ya Tonight 今晚有局) is preserved as contextual metadata but is not used in nutritional scoring. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy lamb data and are approximate. 7. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this food type.
